sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a history of high blood pressure and headaches, which have been ongoing for about a month.
The patient reports headaches on the top of the head, which are throbbing and relieved by Tylenol.
The patient also reports feeling dizzy and experiencing blurry vision.
The patient has a history of high blood pressure, diabetes, and is currently taking metformin.
The patient admits to not regularly taking their medication for high blood pressure due to stomach discomfort.
The patient consumes a high-sodium diet, mentioning a frequent consumption of McDonald's.
The patient's blood sugar has been high, with a recent reading of 170 mg/dL, which is below the 200 mg/dL threshold but approaching the borderline limit.
The patient attributes the high blood sugar to consuming Easter candy.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of high blood pressure and diabetes.
Review of systems: The patient reports dizziness, blurry vision, and headaches.
Current medications:
Chlorthalidone (dose not specified) for hypertension (not being taken regularly due to stomach discomfort)
Metformin (dose not specified) for diabetes (will be increased to 1500 mg)
Objective:

Vital signs: Blood pressure 172/98, heart rate within normal limits, oxygenation normal, no jugular venous distention, no carotid bruits, clear lungs, 2/6 systolic ejection murmur on heart exam, 1+ pitting edema on legs bilaterally.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has uncontrolled hypertension, multifactorial in nature, requiring a medication regimen change and dietary modifications.
Problem list: Hypertension; Diabetes; High blood sugar.
Plan:
I will continue the patient's current medication regimen, including increasing the metformin dose to 1500 mg.
I will encourage the patient to take their medication regularly, possibly setting reminders on their iPhone.
I will monitor the patient's blood pressure three times a week and recommend a home blood pressure monitor.
I will schedule a follow-up appointment in one month to reassess the patient's blood pressure control.
I will refer the patient to a nutritionist to discuss dietary changes to reduce sodium intake.
Follow-up: The patient will be scheduled for a one-month follow-up appointment to reassess their blood pressure control and adjust their treatment plan as necessary.
